Tomato___Bacterial_spot.name: Bacterial spot
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Small, dark, water-soaked lesions appear on leaves, stems, and fruit.
prevention: Use disease-free seed and transplants
Rotate crops away from tomatoes
Avoid overhead irrigation and wet foliage
Sanitize tools and remove infected plant debris
treatments: Copper-based bactericides or bactericidal sprays
Prune or remove severely infected leaves and fruit
Improve air circulation in the canopy

Tomato___Early_blight.name: Early blight
Tomato___Early_blight.description: Dark, concentric lesions form on older leaves and stems, causing leaf drop.
prevention: Rotate crops and avoid planting tomatoes after potatoes
Use resistant varieties when available
Avoid overhead watering and dense planting
Mulch soil to reduce splash dispersal
treatments: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil or mancozeb
Remove lower leaves when disease appears
Maintain good plant nutrition and spacing

Tomato___Late_blight.name: Late blight
Tomato___Late_blight.description: Rapidly spreading water-soaked lesions on leaves and fruit, often with white mold on undersides.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and keep foliage dry
Destroy volunteer tomato and potato plants
Plant resistant varieties where possible
Space plants for good air movement
treatments: Use approved fungicides on a regular schedule
Immediately remove and destroy infected plants
Practice strict sanitation of tools and equipment

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.name: Leaf mold
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces and olive-green fuzzy mold on undersides.
prevention: Provide good air circulation and avoid overcrowding
Water in the morning to allow foliage to dry
Use disease-free transplants
Maintain low humidity in greenhouses and tunnels
treatments: Apply fungicides such as copper formulations or chlorothalonil
Remove infected leaves and dispose of them safely
Avoid watering the foliage directly

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.name: Septoria leaf spot
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Small, circular spots with dark margins and light centers appear on lower leaves.
prevention: Rotate crops and avoid planting in infected soil
Mulch around plants to limit soil splash
Space plants for airflow
Remove and destroy infected leaves
treatments: Use fungicides labeled for Septoria leaf spot
Practice good sanitation by removing debris
Keep lower foliage dry

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.name: Spider mites (Two-spotted spider mite)
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Fine webbing and tiny yellow or bronze speckles appear on leaves, causing leaf bronzing and drop.
prevention: Maintain adequate humidity and avoid plant stress
Inspect plants regularly for early mite activity
Encourage natural predators like predatory mites
Keep greenhouse and production areas clean
treatments: Use miticides approved for tomato crops
Apply insecticidal soaps or horticultural oils
Rinse leaves with strong water spray to reduce mite populations

Tomato___Target_Spot.name: Target spot
Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Brown to tan lesions with a yellow halo appear on leaves and may spread rapidly.
prevention: Rotate crops and remove plant debris
Avoid overhead irrigation
Space plants to increase airflow
Use resistant varieties if available
treatments: Apply registered fungicides on a labeled schedule
Remove severely infected foliage
Practice good field sanitation

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.name: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Leaves curl upward, turn yellow, and stunted growth occurs; fruit yield drops sharply.
prevention: Use resistant or tolerant varieties
Control whitefly vectors with screens or treatments
Remove infected plants promptly
Avoid planting near infected crops
treatments: No cure exists; remove infected plants
Manage whiteflies with insecticides or biological controls
Use healthy transplants and barrier screens

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.name: Tomato mosaic virus
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Mottled light and dark green leaf patterns, stunted plants, and reduced fruit quality.
prevention: Use virus-free seed and transplants
Sanitize tools and hands between plants
Remove infected plants immediately
Avoid tobacco products while handling tomatoes
treatments: No chemical cure; rogue infected plants
Maintain strict sanitation practices
Use resistant varieties when available

Tomato___healthy.name: Healthy
Tomato___healthy.description: No disease detected; the plant appears healthy and free of major symptoms.
prevention: Continue good cultural practices
Maintain proper watering and nutrition
Monitor regularly for pests and disease
Use good sanitation and crop rotation
